text: Surface ion layer distribution at the air/water interface holds crucial importance for numerous environmental phenomena. It has been previously suggested that larger ions tend to display more hydrophobic behavior and as such are adsorbed at the surface. This adsorption will yield significant electric fields that dictate interfacial water arrangement. However, in this study, surface-specific heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD VSFS) in combination with neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations are employed to investigate ion positions within electrolyte solutions. Consequently, distribution of ions within electrolyte solutions is determined, and surface interfaces are stratified into two different water environments. The outermost water environment is ion depleted, while ion enrichment is observed in the subsurface layer. The ion-induced structural rearrangement in interfacial water, observed at the air/water interface, is attributed to such surface stratification.
